What the Server Is Doing During Inference

Lab 0 field guide — reading the RTX 3080 Ti inference server's three monitors while a client streams LLM requests. Every value below was read directly from the capture frames (120 of them).

The whole story in one line: a request arrives over the network → the GPU reads the prompt in one big burst (prefill) and emits the first token (that whole span is your TTFT) → then it writes the rest of the answer one token at a time (decode = your TPS / TPOT) → it goes quiet until the next request. Each tool below shows that same cycle from a different angle.

The three things you are looking at

PaneToolThe question it answers
GPUNVIDIA app → Performance → StatisticsIs the GPU working, how hard, and is it healthy (clock, power, heat, voltage)?
SYSTEMTask Manager → PerformanceIs anything else — CPU, RAM, disk — the bottleneck? (It should not be.)
SERVERLM Studio → Developer → Local ServerIs the model loaded, the API serving, and how many requests are in flight right now?

The GPU vital signs (NVIDIA app) — what each tile means

TileWhat it representsWhy it changes during inference
GPU ClockCore compute frequency (MHz)Boosts to ~1800–2000 MHz while a request runs; coasts down (to 250–850 MHz) in the gaps.
GPU PowerBoard power draw (Watts)Tracks real compute. Peaked at 307 W; the 3080 Ti's reference board power is ~350 W (some board models allow more), so 307 W leaves headroom — it is not power-capped. The most reliable "is it busy" signal.
GPU TemperatureDie temperature (°C)Follows power: 63–75 °C. Throttling starts ~83 °C, so it is healthy.
GPU VoltageCore voltage (V)Moves with the clock (P-state): ~0.75 V coasting, up to ~1.08 V at full boost.
VRAM ClockMemory (GDDR6X) frequency (MHz)Pinned at 9251 MHz almost the whole test. Decode hammers memory, so memory stays at full speed while serving (see "memory-bandwidth-bound" below).
GPU Utilization% of the last sample window a kernel ranCoarse, instantaneous. Flips between ~80–90% (request running) and 0–12% (gap). See caveat below.
GPU Fan SpeedCooling fan (RPM)Barely moved (1302–1351 RPM) — fans react slowly, so a 2-min burst test doesn't spin them up.
CPU UtilizationWhole-system CPU load (%), shown by the NVIDIA appRose to ~30–42% while requests ran, fell to 3–10% in gaps — the CPU tokenizes, samples, and runs the HTTP server. Not the bottleneck.
Accuracy caveat — don't read one frame. "GPU Utilization" is NVIDIA's coarse counter: the fraction of the last sampling slice in which any kernel ran. Several frames show 0% utilization while power is still 277–307 W and the clock is ~1935 MHz — not a contradiction; the 1-second snapshot just landed in the gap right after a burst, before clock and power decayed. Trust the oscillation, and use power as the cleaner activity proxy.

Two terms in plain English

KV cache (key-value cache)

As the model reads your prompt and then writes each new token, it saves the key and value vectors it computed for every token it has already seen (one pair per token, per attention layer) in GPU memory. That saved pile is the KV cache. It lets the model produce the next token by looking only at the newest token plus the cache, instead of re-reading the whole conversation from scratch every time.

Why it matters: without it, generating token #500 would mean re-processing the previous 499 tokens — far slower. The trade-off is memory: the cache grows with conversation length and with the number of simultaneous requests, and it lives in VRAM. So on a 12 GB card it is a major space consumer, and it's why very long prompts or many parallel users can run the GPU out of memory.

Memory-bandwidth-bound

A task is "memory-bandwidth-bound" when its speed is limited by how fast data can be moved from GPU memory (VRAM) into the compute cores — not by how fast the cores can do math. Generating one token for a single request requires reading the model's entire set of weights (about 4.7 GB — the Q4_K_M quantized size of this ~7.6B-parameter model) out of VRAM while doing relatively little math with them, so the cores spend much of their time waiting on memory.

Why it matters: it means decode speed (TPOT / TPS) is set mostly by memory bandwidth, not raw compute. The levers that speed up generation are therefore faster memory, quantization (smaller weights = less to move), and batching (reuse one weight read for several requests at once). It's also why the VRAM clock pins to its maximum during decode. (Prefill is the opposite — compute-bound — because it crunches many prompt tokens in parallel.)

All 120 frames — the full time series

Every capture frame, OCR-read from the NVIDIA tiles and plotted across the ~2-minute test. This is the real shape of the load, not a sample.

What the curves say

  • Power climbs to a 307 W peak early, then oscillates 130–285 W — one hump per request burst. The cleanest picture of "the GPU pulsing."
  • Core clock rides 1800–2000 MHz but drops hard to 250–450 MHz in the gaps — the card relaxes between bursts.
  • VRAM clock is a flat line at 9251 MHz: once serving, memory never relaxes — consistent with decode being memory-bandwidth-bound.
  • Temperature rises and falls a few degrees behind power; never near the throttle point.
  • GPU / CPU utilization rise and fall together and cluster at "busy" or "idle" — the on/off pulse of a bursty workload.
How accurate is this OCR? The numbers were machine-read from the screenshots, then validated against 14 frames read by eye. Match quality: GPU clock, power, temperature, voltage, VRAM clock, and fan = 0.0% error; CPU = 5.3%; GPU-utilization = 26% (its small two-digit font drops a leading digit, which is exactly why we lean on power instead). Per-frame data: Performance_Captures_telemetry.csv.

The three panes during the test (relate them to the table below)

All three monitors were captured together on the server. Read them left-to-right, then compare to the 14-frame table that follows.

Life of a request — step by step, and where to see it

  1. 1. Client sends the request (network). An HTTP POST /v1/chat/completions (JSON: messages, max_tokens, stream true) goes to the server on port 1234. On a home LAN this hop is a few milliseconds at most — negligible next to the model's processing time.
  2. 2. LM Studio prepares it (CPU). It applies the chat template and tokenizes the prompt into token IDs. SERVER a new job ID appears; GPU CPU-util ticks up.
  3. 3. Scheduling / batching. With room for concurrent requests, several run together. SERVER that's why two GEN jobs are visible — the GPU serves them as a batch, sharing each weight read.
  4. 4. Prefill — the prompt is read in one burst. The GPU pushes all prompt tokens through the model in one/few batched passes and builds the KV cache. This is compute-bound and the heaviest moment: GPU clock and power spike, util 80–90%; SYSTEM the 3D graph spikes.
  5. 5. First token leaves — TTFT is now defined. The model samples the first token and streams it as an SSE data: chunk. TTFT = time from step 1 to this first token (network + queue + tokenize + prefill + first token).
  6. 6. Decode — the rest streams out, one token at a time (TPS / TPOT). Each new token needs a forward pass that reads all model weights from VRAM plus the growing KV cache — memory-bandwidth-bound, so GPU VRAM clock stays pinned at 9251 MHz; SERVER "GEN … tok" counts up.
  7. 7. Completion. On a stop token or max_tokens, LM Studio sends data: [DONE] and closes. Total = latency = TTFT + decode time.
  8. 8. Gap, then repeat. The slot frees; GPU util drops to 0–12% and the clock coasts down until the next request. That alternation is the row-of-spikes on every chart.

How it all correlates — one cycle, every gauge

StageGPU (NVIDIA)System (Task Mgr)Server (LM Studio)Your KPI
Prefill + first tokenClock & power spike; util high; VRAM 9251CPU rises (tokenize); 3D spikeJob appears, prompt processedTTFT (ends at the first token)
Decode (tokens 2…N)VRAM clock pinned; util high; power steadyCPU moderate; 3D sustained"GEN … tok" counts upTPS / TPOT
Gap (between)Util 0–12%; clock/power coast downCPU 3–10%; 3D dipsNo active job (or the other one)idle time

The first token is itself one decode step, so TTFT ends exactly where TPOT begins.

Latency, inter-token latency, and jitter — definitions

MetricWhat it isHow it's calculated
TTFTWait until the answer starts.t(first token) − t(sent). Includes network + queue + tokenize + prefill + first token.
Total request latencyWait until the answer is complete.t(last token) − t(sent) = TTFT + decode_time.
ITL (inter-token latency)Gap between two consecutive output tokens (a per-token series).ITLₕ = t(token i) − t(token i−1).
TPOTThe average ITL — typical streaming pace.mean(ITL) = decode_time / (N−1); TPS = 1 / mean(ITL).
JitterHow uneven the token stream is (smoothness).stdev(ITL) or p95(ITL) − p50(ITL); SLOs use p95 ITL.

Putting it together: Latency = TTFT + (N−1) × TPOT. ITL and jitter need a timestamp on every streamed token; the scripts now capture this.

Your questions, answered precisely

  1. Isn't TTFT more than prefill? Yes. TTFT = time to the first token = network + queue + tokenize + prefill + the first token (one decode step). Prefill dominates but isn't the whole of it. Cleanly: Latency = TTFT + (N−1) × TPOT.
  2. Why does the CPU tokenize, not the GPU? Tokenization is string parsing (byte-pair-encoding merges + vocabulary lookups) — small, sequential work CPUs do well; GPUs are for big parallel matrix math. It's cheap next to the forward pass.
  3. Where is embedding? Two things: (a) inside the LLM, each token ID is looked up in the model's embedding matrix to become a vector — on the GPU, folded into prefill; (b) RAG embeddings are a separate model for vector-DB search — not used in this chat test.
  4. Does "GEN … tok" mean output tokens? Yes — the request is in the generation (decode) phase and the number is output tokens produced so far.
  5. Why map decode to TPS/TPOT and not TTFT? TTFT is measured once (the first token); TPS/TPOT describe the ongoing stream of tokens 2…N. They meet at the first-token boundary.
The carrier-class lesson. Under a light, bursty load the GPU still has gaps — it isn't fully fed, so utilization averages well below 100%. Add more concurrent clients and raise the request rate and the gaps close: utilization rides near 100%, power sits near the card's limit, the VRAM clock stays maxed, and continuous batching packs more requests onto the same weight reads (raising aggregate throughput). The instant requests arrive faster than the GPU can drain them, a queue forms and TTFT climbs while per-request TPS falls. Finding that knee — how many concurrent users one GPU can serve within your latency SLO — is the whole point of capacity testing.

Quick reference — which gauge answers which question

You want to know…Look at…
Is the GPU actually doing the work?GPU Utilization + GPU Clock (NVIDIA) and the 3D graph (Task Manager) — over several seconds, not one frame
Am I watching prefill (TTFT) or decode (TPS)?Prefill = biggest compute/power spike; decode = VRAM clock pinned high while "GEN tok" counts up
How many requests are in flight?LM Studio "Loaded Models" job tiles
Is the GPU healthy / not throttling?Temperature (< ~83 °C) and Power (below the card's limit)
Is something other than the GPU the limit?CPU %, Memory, Disk in Task Manager (all stayed low/steady)

Values read from all 120 capture frames (19:55:38–19:57:53) of a single-client continuous-load test, model qwen2.5-coder-7b-instruct on an RTX 3080 Ti (12 GB). Continuous metrics validated to 0.0% error against hand-read frames; GPU-utilization is NVIDIA's coarse instantaneous counter (use as a trend). Power-limit (~350 W) and throttle-point (~83 °C) are reference figures for this GPU class. No addresses, hostnames, or credentials are shown.
